speaker: interviewer
text: This is August 9, 1995. The survivor we are interviewing is Edith Danko, born Hershkowitz. The interviewer is Nina Dorf. We are in Morton Grove, Illinois, United States of America. And the language we're using for this interview is English. This is Nina Dorf, and today we are interviewing Edith Danko, born Edith Hershkowitz. This is August 6, 1995. We're interviewing in Morton Grove, Illinois, United States of America. And we're interviewing in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Danko, can you tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Edith Danko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. E-D-E-I-T-H Danko-- D-A-N-K-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name maid is Hershowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. [? H-E-R-S-T-K-O-T-Z. ?] Hershkowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good. And your birth date.

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is 1922, October 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 53-- 73. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good. And what town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Seleszcze. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [? S-E-L-E-S-Z-C-Z-E. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what country at the time you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Great. Now we can use this beginning time to talk about your growing up years, what kind of a town it was, and your family. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was brought up, it was eight children in the house, four boys and four girls. My parents was now young, both of them. It was very religious in house. I couldn't take a brush on Sabbath-- on Saturday-- to brush my hair. My father wouldn't let me do this. And I just agreed with him. It's for Shabbas. And we was all [? in ?] just [? guard ?] [? of ?] two brothers. They was in school. One brother was in gymnasium. The other was just learning, like, I don't know, like a shoemaker. And my oldest sister was married. She got already four children. And then between this was my father in the war. This was now before the first war, in 1916 or 1917, in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I know-- I understand that you started talking about how old everybody was at the time the Second World War started. But let's go back. And I know that you want to talk about your dad, so go ahead and tell the story about your father. And then we'll talk about the kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father-- I mean, they married both very young. My mother was 16, my father was 17 when they married. And then they got three children. And then my father was in the war, the first war, 19-- I think 1916 or the '17. He was young. My mother was home with the children. And then, once he was there, stay in the night. And somebody come and told him, he said, you are [? not ?] young. You go home and lay down and sleep. And I will stay. I will take your-- your place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father went up a few steps, and then he hear a shot. And he run back, and the man was dead. And he just-- he says, he came by himself. And he wanted-- he want to take the place of my father. My father was very upset of this. Well, he was supposed to be there. And the-- a man was there. But he could do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the same time, my mother got a dream that [? him's ?] mother said it's the day. I don't know how many years like I died. You even light a candle after me. And now I'm stand. And I'm watching your husband on the war. My mother was scared even to go down from bed to make the light. [? Other ?] still-- and she got a sister that was staying with her. And she said-- asked the sister-- go down and make the light. And she start to come, and then she saw that is her [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], like when she died. And then after he came home-- I don't know, about two years or the three years that he came home-- then they got another girl. She died when she was two years old, and then I was born. And they named me after her. After me was now three children. Then we was eight in the family, four brothers and four sisters. And we all lived-- it was OK. I mean, it was happy, and we got everything what we needed. We didn't need it, to nobody nothing. We worked with-- we'd ask and we got, and we know that's-- we didn't know from better, that's it. It was, maybe, about 50 or the 60 Jewish families there, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was big, but the Jewish people was not too many there. And it was big, and it was most Ukrainian and Czechs was living. Czechs not as much, like the Ukrainian of us lived. We all of us living together, working, and doing everything, and talking and get together. Everything was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me, you said it was-- you lived on a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a big farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there also a village where people didn't live on farms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody lived on farms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was living on farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because a lot of them didn't got nothing other they lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a lot of-- from the farm, it's got a lot of-- I don't know how to explain it. First was wheat, a lot of. And that was what was grown, potatoes, corn, everything what you needed in the farms. And we used--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you grew everything that you needed for your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's-- it was enough for our family. Well, it was enough, but some my father used to give away, and they bought, the Ukrainian, from us. And a third they took for them, and the others they get for us, what was grown there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was given to people who worked for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was given. But we couldn't-- we couldn't work this, to do the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father do another job besides the farming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was buying, like, food and selling, and with horses buying and selling. That's what he used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So an animal and food broker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like this, yeah. Most fruit, but he used to buy and to sell to, like, the Czechoslovakian there in Prague or the other states. And that's what we did. We got all of this enough, what we needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know this is a silly question with eight children, but did your mother have a job also besides the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, no. Not once of us. I didn't get a job. I was-- I didn't work too much. Well, I got older or whatever, they did the job. I didn't do too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother took care of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took care of the house, and she took care of the children. With cooking and baking, she got enough. She used to have now sometimes help in house, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have help in the house where someone lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody lived with us. Although through the day, somebody used to come in and to help them. And like not just in house, but the outside mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you probably helped too. Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped too. Oh, sure. Although not as much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do too much. I don't know. I was lazy. The first time that I worked was here in the United States. I didn't know a woman had to go into work. I know the girls worked for us, they have to work, because they didn't got what they needed. Some reason. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Or that I didn't-- I didn't need it, to go into work to make money. We got enough and everything. We used to give now for poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. That's really interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us. My mother used to send me to go and to drop off milk or the sour cream, things. Potatoes. I used always to give-- they used always to give away mine to poor, the [? chicken. ?] Or the children, my sister and brothers, used to tell me I shouldn't eat. Well, I don't have mine in there to put of the table. Oh, they always, every year, that's what they give. They give away eggs, a lot of things. And all of us did. My mother used to send me, go ahead, go. They don't have what to eat. Take a bread. Take this, and go give the people. Let them have what we eat. Let them have breakfast. With a lot of things. She helped-- she helped out with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was one thing you did for the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about helping in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to help. I used to do in the house, although not-- not a lot. Not a lot. When my sister married, she got the children. I used to be most with her to help her with the children. I liked to play with them, run. I was-- And I liked to be with my sister in the house. Well, she got four children. She was not young, and it was very hard of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she lived in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, she got a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far. Not far from us, oh, no. She got a beautiful house. She got a nice husband. They was happy and got nice children. The oldest was, I think, 13 years. And there was three small little-- and there's nobody left from them. And that's what-- and I used a lot of help by my grandmother, going to her and to help her in house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother live nearby also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, that part of the-- she got her own--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose mother was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father's parent, I didn't know. They named me the second. I was already named after my grandmother. I didn't know my father's parents, although my mother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother's mother lived nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as close, although she was-- she got her house. She got everything what she needed. She got, in the time, two sons in United States. They used to send and help a lot of-- for her, although she got eight girls. It was not easy to get-- to marry eight girls there, although they all was married, and they all was OK. And my grandma was OK. My grandpa died before the war, and then she was alone, although she was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you used to go help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to help her [INAUDIBLE]. And the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just to be with her. Just to be-- to sleep overnight there by her. Just to be. I did-- Like I said, I was lazy. I didn't work too much. And I [? stayed ?] there. My mother worked all [? out. ?] When I needed, OK, I did work too, although not as much. I was two times in Prague to visit. I got an aunt there. I was staying with her. I got a Romanian aunt, and I was visiting the aunt, and-- I mean, before the war-- to visit them. And I got a easy life, even though it was a farm. Maybe it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help with the farm work?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of times, sure. Like by the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you use to do on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I used to-- you know what, I used to like to-- the potatoes, to take out. That's what I used to like to do. I'd like to go in and to look around, how was it growing and everything. And in the garden I worked all right, every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I like to work in the garden. Other so, I didn't went in the farm out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you milk the cows?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got a few of them. Never I did this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It smelled. And we couldn't even drink the milk, how long they didn't brought the milk. Couldn't take the smell from the cow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the chickens and other animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the chickens, I didn't got nothing. I wouldn't check if they got an egg inside. Other to picking up the eggs after them, this I did, sure. I feed them. I liked to-- to play with them, to throw food, and they run after me for more food. That's not important, yes. It was-- like I said, it was everything was going OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was four years in the Ukraine school and two years in Czech school That's what I was school, not more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they speak in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, the Ukrainian. And then when we went in Czech school, they speak Czechoslovakian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you speak both of those languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to, sure. That's what I was speaking, and then I got-- the rabbi used to come in house and teach me Jewish, to read and to write, and all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yiddish or in Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish. Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. We just speak Yiddish. And outside, we speak with the neighbors Ukrainian. That was our second language. And then when we would get together with the Czechs, we'd speak Czechoslovakian with them. And it was not-- I mean, we understood each other. We understood very good each other. Through the war and after, I saw how wrong I was to trust them with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before the war, you really trusted your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh yes. I got girlfriends and friends. In school, we was going together, everything. We used to sharing things. But then during the war, when they start, it was changing overnight. One girl, she was working in office. There-- OK, here, I have to on already, when was the war. I was in-- in the ghetto. She was coming there. She wanted to help me to run, and then I run away in the ghetto. I didn't want it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go back to school a little bit. Do you have any special memories of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have not for myself. I have from my brother. But he was-- got beaten up, and he was a good student. Just while he was-- the teacher was against Jews. And I can't forget. He come with such a swollen hand. He beat him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have-- there were some times before the war that you remember some bad things that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Not too many. Not too many. I remember more good things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what good things do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that we was together, used to help each other. If they-- it was like the Shabbat, they saw it's raining. We got something outside. They just came and they took them. They know I-- we were not doing this. They helped us and we helped them. We did not to hating each other. That's what I think. I didn't know to hate them. I was thinking they're just-- just like we are. No difference. It looks-- to go on here, they used to make weddings, to inviting each other. I mean, not to the dinner, not for this, although not in the synagogues to invite them. But then we was always like-- like family with them. Everybody was close, and when we need something, we went to them. If they need something, they came to us to ask for a favor. But we didn't know some-- how-- how they feel about us. Well, I didn't know that in a few hours, they could feel like they did. I wouldn't believe this. It was very hard. It was hard to take it

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about some of the happier times a little bit more. Do you remember any of the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. I remember these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday? You know, all of us very-- would be to keep up the holidays, the Shabbat. We didn't do nothing on Saturday. We didn't work on the holidays. We cooked the food that's what's supposed to be for Rosh Hashanah and for Pesach. The matzahs we baked. We baked ourself matzahs. You baked matzahs yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We used to get together, all the Jewish of one place, and make, like, for Passover, and we used to make the matzahs. A lot of baking. I mean, we was going there and-- to make the matzahs, and everybody-- it was not of the pound or of the kilo. We make just 200 matzahs for one family, or the one-- or the 300 matzahs. And we used to bake them ourselves, bread ourselves, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You baked bread at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We baked bread at home. Everything-- challahs, bread. Everything was-- everything was homemade. We didn't know nothing to go to buy. Not forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get at your kosher meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we got a shochet.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the village. Oh, sure, we got a shochet in the village. And he was-- once a week for Shabbat, Thursday, he used to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], to kill this. And then through the week, if we wanted chicken to have or something, then we went to him, other he came over and he did it. Thursday, he used to go in in every village. He was-- he used to live in our village, although he was to go in three villages. The same shochet. And whatever was prepared, cows--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the shochet that we saw in the movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the shochet that we saw in the film?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't show on the film. Him son lived in Florida. I talk-- I talked with him a long time ago. He was a nice, nice shochet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me some more, what you remember about the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go into the shul in the morning, Daven and prayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a shul in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a big shul, the room we was sitting. It was meet-- a [? matzav. ?] It was woman next door, and the men used to sit next to it. And my mom-- mother was so religious. She always scared she didn't pray enough. She used to come home-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hard to talk about your parents. That's why we put the Kleenex here. OK? So don't feel bad about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was very-- when I speak about it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father-- we was eight children in the house. He wouldn't-- he never hurt nobody from us. He used to howl at us, don't do this, don't make noise. He would never touched us.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of you ever got spanked?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My mother, yes. She-- she was a little different with-- well, she was in house the whole day with us. Although my father, he wouldn't kill a fly on the wall. He was so honest, and died so ugly. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But it's over, that. And died young and ugly, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they when they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was both young. Something in the 50, maybe. I don't know exactly. Well, they married young. They got children. They got grandchildren already. I don't know. They was young. They was maybe like-- I don't know. I cannot think now, even, how old they can-- could be. He was nice, tall, and skinny, my father. And always with a smile. And I see my son, I always think about my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son looks like your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he look just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I told him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to talk about, about your years as a child, before we go on to talking about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. I know we got a warm house and everything what we needed. Share it with other people. And they was both very nice, religious. My father, he really knew what God's telling you to do. God will help you. OK. We did-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We did everything what father asked us to do. And he said if you do, God will help you. My mother used to tell us the same thing. It didn't work out like this. Yeah. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you went to school for six years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were at home helping out until the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. It was just what-- I was visiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Prague. When the Germans came into Prague, then I took-- next day, I tooked off and I went home. I got-- my mother got a sister there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger sister was living in Prague. I was a few times visiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop the tape now, and then we'll go on to the next tape. We'll start right here, OK?
